sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 45-year-old male, presents with a 3-week history of right elbow pain, which worsened with movement and improved with rest.
He describes the pain as sharp, stabbing, and throbbing, rating it an 8/10 when using the arm and 3/10 when resting.
The pain is localized to the outside part of the elbow and does not radiate to other areas.
He denies any recent trauma or feeling a pop in the elbow.
He has tried taking Tylenol and ibuprofen, but they do not provide significant relief.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of type 2 diabetes, managed with metformin 500mg twice daily, and mild intermittent asthma, managed with flovent and albuterol as needed.
He has a known allergy to penicillin.
He underwent a cholecystectomy 10 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient's asthma is reported to be under good control, with no recent exacerbations.
Current medications: Metformin 500mg twice daily; Flovent; Albuterol as needed.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient's cardiovascular exam is normal, with a grade 3/6 systolic ejection murmur.
The musculoskeletal exam reveals moderate lateral epicondylar tenderness of the right elbow, with pain on passive range of motion and mild pain with resisted extension of the wrist.
There is no palpable joint effusion.
Test Results: The patient's right elbow X-ray shows no acute fracture or other bony abnormality, with no malalignment or joint effusion.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow, also known as tennis elbow, likely due to overuse from increased tennis activity.
Problem list: Tennis elbow; Diabetes type 2; Asthma.
Plan:
I prescribed Ultram 50mg once daily for the management of right elbow pain.
I recommended the patient to rest the right arm as much as possible, avoid repetitive stress in the right arm, and ice the right elbow for 20 minutes at least 3 times a day.
I also ordered a hemoglobin A1c level, a CBC blood test, and a chem-12 blood test to assess the patient's diabetes control.
I increased the patient's metformin to 1000mg twice daily to better manage his diabetes.
I also wrote a prescription for a refill of albuterol for the patient's asthma.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in 4 weeks to reevaluate the elbow and consider additional imaging or a physical therapy referral if necessary.
